text: The surgeon pre-marks the ostomy site in the right lower quadrant and makes a small incision. A varus needle is used to insufflate the abdomen, followed by the insertion of several trocars. Laparoscopic exploration of the abdomen is performed, and dissection begins by taking the vessels of the diseased sigmoid colon medially. A mesenteric window is created, and dissection proceeds distally towards the recto sigmoid junction. A laparoscopic stapler is deployed to transect the colon, leaving a visible rectal stump.

The surgeon dissects along the descending colon towards the splenic flexure, initially taking the lateral attachments using a combination of energy and cutting. The mesentery is then dissected with an energy device, such as a ligature. The lesser sac is opened, and dissection proceeds from proximal to distal along the transverse colon, with the stomach visible to the left, the pancreas below, and the spleen ahead. Blunt dissection and energy are used to meet the prior dissection at the splenocolic ligament, completing the mobilization of the splenic flexure.

Following the mobilization of the splenic flexure, the surgeon focuses on the hepatic flexure, with the distal portion of the stomach visible to the right and the gallbladder ahead. The surgeon prepares to take the ileocolic pedicle using a ligature device, with the pulsating vessel visible just ahead of the device. Enlarged lymph nodes around the ileocolic pedicle are noted, indicative of inflammatory bowel disease.

The surgeon carefully dissects through the mesentery of the right colon, avoiding the duodenum and right ureter. The remaining lateral attachments are transected, and the transverse colonic mesentery is exposed. The cut edge of the mesentery near the duodenum is identified and transected from the proximal transverse colon towards the splenic flexure, maintaining hemostasis with the ligature device. The final piece of mesentery is controlled, completing the laparoscopic dissection of the colon.

The surgeon places a small wound protector at the future ostomy site and secures it. The colon is carefully extracted through this area, ensuring no spillage or contamination occurs. A stapler is fired across the terminal ileum, and the specimen is removed from the field. The extracted specimen shows severe disease in the distal colon with relative sparing of the proximal colon, characteristic of ulcerative colitis.

The surgeon constructs the end ileostomy using the Brook technique, turning the end of the ileum back and suturing it to the skin to create a smooth, rounded stoma. The correct orientation is confirmed laparoscopically before proceeding.
The video shows the final result of the ileostomy with the ostomy bag covering the stoma on the abdominal wall. A temporary drain is visible to the side of the ostomy bag.
